request.question: The soundtrack to 'Dancer in the Dark' is by what Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ?

title: Björk
Where Is the Line
Dancer in the Dark
List of songs recorded by Björk
I've Seen It All
Jófríður Ákadóttir
Greta Salóme Stefánsdóttir
Innocence (Björk song)
Michael Flessas
Selmasongs
sentences: Björk Guðmundsdóttir (] , born 21 November 1965), known as Björk ( ), is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ.

Over her four-decade career, she has developed an eclectic musical style that draws on a wide range of influences and genres spanning electronic, pop, experimental, classical, trip hop, IDM, and avant-garde styles.
She initially became known as the lead singer of the alternative rock band The Sugarcubes, whose 1987 single "Birthday" was a hit on US and UK indie stations and a favorite among music critics.

Björk embarked on a solo career in 1993, coming to prominence as a solo artist with albums such as "Debut" (1993), "Post" (1995), and "Homogenic" (1997), while collaborating with a range of artists and exploring a variety of multimedia projects.
"Where Is the Line" is a song recorded by Icelandic singer Björk for her sixth studio album "Medúlla".
It was written by the singer herself and co-produced with longtime collaborator Mark Bell.

The track was originally intended to be released as the album's third commercial single, but its release was cancelled just before Björk released the "Drawing Restraint 9" soundtrack, in order to focus all promotional efforts on the film and soundtrack instead.
Some promotional white labels vinyls were released around 2005.
A music video for the song was made by Icelandic Artist Gabríela Friðriksdóttir.
Upon the cancellation of the single, the video was used as part of a video installation project by Friðriksdóttir.
Dancer in the Dark (Danish: Danser i mørket) is a 2000 Danish musical drama film directed by Lars von Trier.

It stars Icelandic singer Björk as a daydreaming immigrant factory worker who suffers from a degenerative eye condition and is saving up to pay for an operation to prevent her young son from suffering the same fate.
Catherine Deneuve, David Morse, Cara Seymour, Peter Stormare, Siobhan Fallon Hogan, and Joel Grey also star.
Icelandic singer and songwriter Björk has recorded more than one hundred songs for eight studio albums, two soundtrack albums, a compilation album, five remix albums and two collaboration albums.
She is the sole writer and producer of most of the songs included in her albums.
She also sometimes plays instruments during her recording sessions.
Moreover, she has provided credited and uncredited contributions on songs recorded by other artists, including background vocals, songwriting, remixing and production.
"I've Seen It All" is a song by Icelandic singer Björk, with lyrics by Sjón and Lars von Trier.
It was released as the first promotional single from the "Dancer in the Dark" soundtrack, "".
The song features vocals from Radiohead frontman Thom Yorke.
It was nominated for an Academy Award for Best Original Song.
Lyrically, it speaks of one coming to terms with the fact that they are going blind.
Jófríður Ákadóttir, known professionally as JFDR, is an Icelandic singer, songwriter and multi-instrumentalist born in 1994.
She is known for her solo project JFDR as well as her work with Samaris, Pascal Pinon and Gangly and works closely with producer and multi-instrumentalist .
Her father is Icelandic composer and musician Áki Ásgeirsson.
Greta Salóme Stefánsdóttir (] ; born 11 November 1986), known outside Iceland simply as Greta Salóme (] ), is an Icelandic singer, songwriter, and violinist in the Icelandic Symphony Orchestra.
She represented Iceland in the Eurovision Song Contest 2012 in Baku, Azerbaijan, in a duet with singer Jónsi with the song "Never Forget".
They qualified from the first semi-final into the Eurovision final and eventually finished in 20th place with 46 points.
She represented Iceland again, this time as a soloist, in the Eurovision Song Contest 2016 with the song "Hear Them Calling", where she failed to qualify.
"Innocence" is a song written and recorded by Icelandic singer Björk.
The song was released as the second single from her seventh full-length studio album, "Volta".
It is co-produced by record producer Timbaland and his protégé Danja.
The song was premiered on Björk's MySpace profile on March 19, 2007 as part of the fan contest to submit ideas for the music video.
The single was released exclusively as a digital download on July 23, 2007.
Michael C. Flessas (born June 2, 1959 in Miami, Florida), is the birth name of American actor Michael Flessas, who is of Greek ancestry.
Flessas' most notable film role was "Angry Man" in the Cannes Film Festival 2000 Palme d'Or winning film "Dancer in the Dark" directed by Danish film director Lars von Trier.
Originally, the director himself considered playing the role but, instead, the role was given to Flessas.
"Dancer in the Dark" starred Icelandic singer/actress Björk who won the Best Actress award at Cannes for her role.

French film icon, César Award winner, and Academy Award nominee Catherine Deneuve, and other noteworthy artists such as Academy Award and Tony Award winner Joel Grey, Peter Stormare, David Morse, and Stellan Skarsgård also performed in the multiple prize winning film.
One of Björk's songs for the film received an Academy Award nomination for Best Song.
Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack 'Dancer in the Dark' is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk.
It was released on September 18, 2000, by One Little Indian Records to promote and accompany the film "Dancer in the Dark".
In the film Björk starred as Selma Ježková, a Czech immigrant who has moved to the United States.
The album features classical arrangements, as well as melodies and beats composed of sounds from mundane objects, such as factory machines and trains.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

hop1_docs: Mary J. Blige | Mary Jane Blige ( ; born January 11, 1971) is an American singer, songwriter, rapper, model, record producer and actress.  Starting her career as a backing singer on Uptown Records in 1989, Blige released her first album, "What's the 411? ", in 1992, and has released 13 studio albums since and made over 150 guest appearances on other albums and soundtracks.

Selmasongs | Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack 'Dancer in the Dark' is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk.  It was released on September 18, 2000, by One Little Indian Records to promote and accompany the film "Dancer in the Dark".  In the film Björk starred as Selma Ježková, a Czech immigrant who has moved to the United States.  The album features classical arrangements, as well as melodies and beats composed of sounds from mundane objects, such as factory machines and trains.

Samurai Champloo Music Record: Departure | Samurai Champloo Music Record: Departure is a soundtrack album, released on June 23, 2004, for the anime series "Samurai Champloo".  It is the second of four soundtrack albums released for the show.  The album was produced by Japanese DJ/producer Nujabes, and American MC/producer Fat Jon, and featured Japanese rapper Shing02 and Japanese singer-songwriter MINMI on the album's first and last tracks, respectively.
List of awards and nominations received by Anahí | Anahí is a Mexican singer-songwriter, actress, record producer and dancer.

Vespertine | Vespertine is the fourth solo album by Icelandic musician Björk, released on 27 August 2001, on One Little Indian Records.  It was recorded at locations in Spain, Iceland, and the United States in 2000; production began during the filming of "Dancer in the Dark", which was characterized by conflict between the singer and director Lars von Trier. " Björk", a self-titled coffee table book containing photographs of the singer throughout her career, was released simultaneously with the album.  "Vespertine"' s sound reflected Björk's newly found interest in the music of artists such as Opiate and Console, who were also enlisted as producers.
Charlotte Devaney | Charlotte Devaney is a British DJ, producer, singer, dancer and actress.

Howard Donald | Howard Paul Donald (born 28 April 1968), is an English singer, songwriter, drummer, pianist, dancer, DJ and record producer.  He is a member of English pop-group Take That and a judge on the German reality talent show "Got to Dance".

response.summary_1:
Primary topics: Selmasongs (Dancer in the Dark soundtrack) and Björk (Icelandic musician/film star).

- Passage facts: "Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack 'Dancer in the Dark' is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk."  
- Passage facts: "'released on September 18, 2000, by One Little Indian Records'."  
- Passage facts: "In the film Björk starred as Selma Ježková."  
- Passage facts: "'production began during the filming of "Dancer in the Dark"'" (from Vespertine entry linking Björk to the film's production period).

Ambiguity / missing facts: The passages call her an "Icelandic musician" and show she "starred" in the film, but they do NOT verbatim list the occupations in the question ("singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, and DJ") nor provide a full legal/name variant (e.g., "Björk Guðmundsdóttir"). Bridge entity needed: Björk — confirm occupational labels and full name.

Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
1. Fetch the "Björk" article — lede/infobox and "Career"/"Filmography" sections to confirm occupations (look for "singer, songwriter, actress, record producer, DJ") and full name variants.
2. Fetch the "Selmasongs" article — lede/infobox and "Background"/"Release" sections to confirm soundtrack credit wording.
3. Search: "Björk occupations singer songwriter record producer DJ actress" (or "Björk Guðmundsdóttir lede occupations") if direct page sections are insufficient.
4. If needed, check "Vespertine" article lede/production section for additional linkage to the film's production.
hop2_docs: Pummilla Tallinnaan | "Pummilla Tallinnaan" is a song by Finnish rapper Tuomas Kauhanen featuring Mikko (full name Mikko Björk).  The song peaked at number one on the Finnish Singles Chart.

Wanderlust (Björk song) | "Wanderlust" is a song written and recorded by Icelandic singer Björk.  The track was released digitally as the fourth single from her seventh full-length studio album, "Volta", on 7 April 2008.

Innocence (Björk song) | "Innocence" is a song written and recorded by Icelandic singer Björk.  The song was released as the second single from her seventh full-length studio album, "Volta".  It is co-produced by record producer Timbaland and his protégé Danja.  The song was premiered on Björk's MySpace profile on March 19, 2007 as part of the fan contest to submit ideas for the music video.  The single was released exclusively as a digital download on July 23, 2007.

All Is Full of Love | "All Is Full of Love" is a song by Icelandic musician Björk, taken from her third studio album "Homogenic".  Written by herself, the lyrics are inspired by the presence of love in the advent of spring, and Norse mythology's Ragnarök.  The more popular version of the track, the original mix solely produced by Björk, was used in the accompanying music video, but did not appear on the album.  The version used for the record is a remix by Howie B and has a minimalist approach, with it placing emphasis on the singer's vocals.  The video version features a combination of electronic beats and string instruments.  A remix by the German IDM duo Funkstörung was released as a single in the summer of 1998.  "All Is Full of Love" subsequently received a full single release in 7 June 1999 to coincide with the premiere of its music video.
Björk (name) | Björk, Björck, Biörck, or Bjork is a Swedish surname meaning birch.

Declare Independence | "Declare Independence" is a song written and recorded by Icelandic singer Björk.  The track was released as the third single from her seventh full-length studio album, "Volta".  The single was released on 1 January 2008.  Björk's dedication of the song to various independence causes at live performances of the song in Shanghai and Tokyo have caused controversy.
Björk (exhibition) | Björk was an exhibition at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) about the Icelandic singer of the same name.
response.summary_2: Selmasongs: Music from the Motion Picture Soundtrack "Dancer in the Dark" is the first soundtrack album by Icelandic musician Björk. Björk is an Icelandic singer and songwriter.
